label: Great Pacific Garbage Patch
sub: ~79,000 metric tons of plastic across 1.6M km². Primary target for The Ocean Cleanup's oceanic systems.

label: The Ocean Cleanup HQ
sub: Rotterdam, Netherlands. Global headquarters, engineering hub, and home to R&D for ocean and river cleanup technology since 2013.

label: System 001 Launch Site
sub: Alameda, California. System 001 departed from here on September 8, 2018, cheered by over 100,000 livestream viewers.

label: Interceptor 001 – Klang River
sub: Selangor, Malaysia. Deployed October 2019. Solar-powered autonomous river cleanup system in one of Southeast Asia's most plastic-polluted rivers.

label: Interceptor 002 – Cengkareng Drain
sub: Jakarta, Indonesia. Deployed November 2019. Indonesia is the world's 2nd-largest ocean plastic contributor. The Interceptor collects tonnes of plastic weekly.

label: Interceptor 003 – Can Tho River
sub: Can Tho, Vietnam. Part of the Mekong Delta river system, one of the highest-volume plastic-transport waterways in Southeast Asia.

label: Interceptor 004 – Río Ozama
sub: Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic. First Interceptor in the Caribbean, deployed in partnership with the Dominican Republic government.

label: Great Barrier Reef
sub: Queensland, Australia. World's largest coral reef system (344,000 km²). Suffered its 7th mass bleaching event in 2024; 73% of surveyed area bleached.

label: Mesoamerican Reef
sub: Belize/Mexico. World's second-largest barrier reef system. Active coral restoration by SECORE International and local partners; heat-tolerant corals being seeded.

label: Coral Triangle
sub: Indonesia/Philippines/Malaysia. Contains 76% of all known coral species and 37% of reef fish species. Faces threats from bleaching, fishing, and coastal development.

label: Papahānaumokuākea Marine National Monument
sub: Hawaii, USA. 1.5 million km² MPA — one of the world's largest. Designated 2006, expanded 2016. Protects critical habitat for Hawaiian monk seals, sea turtles, and seabirds.

label: Chagos Archipelago / BIOT MPA
sub: British Indian Ocean Territory. 640,000 km² no-take MPA designated 2010. Contains some of the world's most pristine coral reefs; sovereignty contested between UK and Mauritius.

label: Ross Sea MPA
sub: Antarctica. World's largest MPA at 1.55 million km², established by CCAMLR in 2016. Protects one of Earth's last near-pristine ocean ecosystems.

label: North Atlantic Garbage Patch
sub: Sargasso Sea accumulation zone. High concentration of microplastics and macroplastics driven by North Atlantic subtropical gyre circulation. Threatens unique Sargassum seaweed ecosystem.

label: South Pacific Garbage Patch
sub: South Pacific subtropical gyre. Estimated 70,000+ metric tons of plastic floating in this accumulation zone; less studied than GPGP but equally concerning.

label: Mediterranean Sea Plastic Hotspot
sub: Semi-enclosed sea with among the world's highest plastic concentrations: ~1,800 plastic items per km². Receives heavy input from European and North African rivers.

label: Bay of Bengal Plastic Hotspot
sub: Receives massive plastic inputs from the Ganges-Brahmaputra-Meghna river system (India, Bangladesh). Among the world's highest plastic-loading coastal regions.

label: South Georgia & South Sandwich Islands
sub: UK Overseas Territory with 1 million km² MPA. Critical humpback and blue whale feeding ground. Humpback population recovering strongly since end of industrial whaling.

label: Gulf of St. Lawrence – Right Whale Habitat
sub: Canada. Critical foraging ground for critically endangered North Atlantic Right Whales (~356 individuals). Ship speed restrictions and seasonal fishing closures enforced since 2017.

label: Bering Sea – North Pacific Right Whale
sub: Alaska, USA. Critical habitat for the critically endangered North Pacific Right Whale — fewer than 500 individuals estimated. Faces entanglement and ship strike risks.
